items.mistake: Calling L(θ) a probability of θ
items.fix: Say L(θ) measures how well θ explains fixed data; only P(θ | x) from Bayes' theorem (with a prior) is a probability over θ.
items.why: Likelihood is not normalized over parameter space and can exceed 1 for continuous models.
items.example: For one Normal observation x=2 with σ=1, L(μ=2) = f(2|μ=2) ≈ 0.399—not a probability that μ equals 2.

items.mistake: Multiplying likelihoods without an independence assumption
items.fix: Use a joint model f(x_1,…,x_n | θ) that captures dependence, or conditional factors P(x_i | x_{<i}, θ).
items.why: The product rule for likelihood requires independence (or an explicit factorization); silent correlation inflates confidence.
items.example: Ten clicks from one user are not IID Bernoulli trials; L(p) = ∏ p^{y_i}(1-p)^{1-y_i} overstates effective sample size.

items.mistake: Maximizing L(θ) instead of ℓ(θ) in code
items.fix: Maximize log-likelihood or minimize negative log-likelihood; use log-sum-exp for numerical stability.
items.why: Products of hundreds of probabilities underflow to 0 in floating point; gradients of log-likelihood are standard in autodiff.
items.example: 100 Bernoulli factors with p=0.9 each give L ≈ 0.9^100 ≈ 2.7×10^−5, but ℓ = 100·log(0.9) ≈ −10.5 is stable.

items.mistake: Confusing density value f(x | θ) with likelihood when θ is also fixed
items.fix: Identify what is observed (data) and what varies (parameters). Write L(θ) explicitly with x substituted.
items.why: Using the PDF at a point as if it were P(X=x) for continuous X, or swapping which symbol is fixed, breaks both probability and inference.
items.example: N(0,1) density at x=0 is ≈0.399; that is f(0|μ=0), not L(μ) unless you treat x=0 as the observed datum and vary μ.
